title: Search element in sorted rotated array
description: Search for a target element in a sorted rotated array

examples.explanation: Step 1: Check arr[0]=4 ≠ 0, continue. Step 2: Check arr[1]=5 ≠ 0, continue. Step 3: Check arr[2]=6 ≠ 0, continue. Step 4: Check arr[3]=7 ≠ 0, continue. Step 5: Check arr[4]=0 = 0, found at index 4. Return 4.
Step 1: Check arr[0]=3 ≠ 0, continue. Step 2: Check arr[1]=1 ≠ 0, continue. Step 3: Reached end of array, target not found. Return -1.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Search O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the array sequentially to check if it matches the target. Use this when the array is small or as a straightforward baseline, but it does not leverage the sorted rotated property.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Search in Sorted Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear search to find target in sorted rotated array
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the linear search approach. The intuition is straightforward: since we're not leveraging the sorted rotated property, we simply check each element sequentially until we find the target or exhaust the array. This works because any search algorithm must examine elements to determine if they match the target, and linear search guarantees we'll find it if present by checking every position. The algorithm: 1) Initialize index to 0. 2) While index < array length: a) If current element equals target, return index. b) Increment index. 3) Return -1 if loop completes without finding target. Time complexity is O(n) as we potentially examine all n elements. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use constant extra variables. This connects to the full problem by providing a complete working solution, though inefficient for large arrays. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays, off-by-one errors in loop conditions, and not returning -1 when target not found. The linear search pattern is fundamental and works regardless of array properties, making it a reliable baseline.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check arr[0]=4 ≠ 0, continue. Step 2: Check arr[1]=5 ≠ 0, continue. Step 3: Check arr[2]=6 ≠ 0, continue. Step 4: Check arr[3]=7 ≠ 0, continue. Step 5: Check arr[4]=0 = 0, found at index 4. Return 4.
Step 1: Check arr[0]=3 ≠ 0, continue. Step 2: Check arr[1]=1 ≠ 0, continue. Step 3: Reached end of array, target not found. Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sequential Element Comparison
ladders.desc: The loop that compares each array element against target
ladders.explanation: This is the core iterative component of linear search. The intuition: to find if target exists, we must examine elements one by one, comparing each to the target. This works because equality comparison is the fundamental way to identify matching elements. The algorithm: 1) For index from 0 to n-1: a) If arr[index] == target, return index. 2) If loop completes, target not found. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the main search logic; L0 adds array bounds checking and return value handling. Common mistakes: using wrong comparison operator, not handling different data types properly, or breaking loop incorrectly. The pattern is a simple for-loop with early return on match.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10 ≠ 20, continue. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20 = 20, found. Return 1.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5 ≠ 6. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=4 ≠ 6. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3 ≠ 6. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=2 ≠ 6. Loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Equality Check
ladders.desc: Determining if current element matches target
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental comparison operation at the heart of search. The intuition: to find a target, we need to identify when we've encountered it, which requires comparing values for equality. This works because if arr[i] equals target, we've found the target at position i. The algorithm is simply: if arr[i] == target, then success. Time complexity O(1) per comparison. Space O(1). This feeds into L1 as the condition checked in each iteration of the loop. Common mistakes: using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), comparing incompatible types, or not considering floating-point precision issues. The pattern is a straightforward equality check within a conditional statement.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 7 == 7. Step 2: Values equal, return true.
Step 1: Compare 5 == 8. Step 2: Values not equal, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Traversal
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements sequentially using indices
ladders.explanation: This involves iterating through array indices to access each element. The intuition: arrays store elements at consecutive positions, so we can access them using integer indices from 0 to n-1. This works because array indexing provides direct access to any position in constant time. The algorithm: for i in range(0, len(arr)): access arr[i]. Time complexity O(n) to visit all elements. Space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to get each element for comparison. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices, using 1-based indexing incorrectly, or modifying index variable incorrectly in loop. The pattern is a simple for-loop with index variable.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=30.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=4. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=2. Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access
ladders.desc: Retrieving value at specific array index
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation of accessing an array element given its index. The intuition: arrays provide random access to elements using indices, allowing us to retrieve any element in constant time. This works because arrays are contiguous memory blocks where each element's location can be calculated from base address and index. The operation: value = arr[index]. Time complexity O(1). Space O(1). This feeds into L3 as the operation performed at each iteration to get current element. Common mistakes: using negative indices unintentionally, accessing beyond array bounds, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. The pattern is straightforward bracket notation or get method.

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate memory offset for index 1. Step 2: Retrieve value at that position = 20.
Step 1: Calculate memory offset for index 3. Step 2: Retrieve value at that position = 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays
ladders.explanation: This covers what an array is: a contiguous collection of elements, each accessible by index. The intuition: arrays organize data sequentially in memory, making them efficient for storage and access. This works because computer memory is linear, and arrays map directly to this structure. Key properties: fixed size (usually), homogeneous elements, zero-based indexing, constant-time access. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space is O(n) for n elements. This feeds into L4 as the foundation for why element access works. Common beginner mistakes: confusing arrays with lists (in some languages), not understanding index bounds, or thinking arrays dynamically resize. The pattern is declaring an array with specified size and type.

examples.trace: Step 1: Allocate memory for 3 integers. Step 2: Store 5 at position 0. Step 3: Store 10 at position 1. Step 4: Store 15 at position 2.
Step 1: Allocate memory for 2 string references. Step 2: Store 'hello' reference at position 0. Step 3: Store 'world' reference at position 1.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Modified Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Adapt binary search to handle the rotation by comparing the target with mid and endpoints to determine which half is sorted and contains the target. This efficiently reduces the search space by half each step.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Sorted Rotated Array using Modified Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find target in sorted rotated array with O(log n) time complexity.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires searching for a target element in an array that was originally sorted in ascending order but has been rotated at some pivot point. The modified binary search approach efficiently handles the rotation by determining which half of the current search space is properly sorted and whether the target lies within that sorted half. The intuition is that even though the array is rotated, at least one half (left or right of mid) will always be sorted. By comparing the target with the sorted half's endpoints, we can confidently eliminate half of the search space at each step, maintaining O(log n) time complexity. The algorithm begins with standard binary search pointers (left, right). At each iteration, calculate mid. If nums[mid] equals target, return mid. Otherwise, check if the left half (left to mid) is sorted by comparing nums[left] ≤ nums[mid]. If sorted, check if target lies within this sorted range (nums[left] ≤ target < nums[mid]). If yes, search left by updating right = mid-1; else search right by updating left = mid+1. If left half is not sorted, then the right half (mid to right) must be sorted. Check if target lies within this sorted range (nums[mid] < target ≤ nums[right]). If yes, search right by updating left = mid+1; else search left by updating right = mid-1. Continue until left > right, then return -1 for not found. Time complexity is O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space complexity is O(1) using only pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete search routine. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison operators (≤ vs <) leading to missed edge cases, and not handling duplicates (though problem assumes distinct elements). Real code pattern: def search(nums, target): left, right = 0, len(nums)-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] == target: return mid; if nums[left] <= nums[mid]: if nums[left] <= target < nums[mid]: right = mid-1; else: left = mid+1; else: if nums[mid] < target <= nums[right]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 != 0. Left half [4,5,6,7] sorted (4≤7). Target 0 not in [4,7) so search right: left=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 != 0. Left half [0,1,2] sorted? nums[4]=0 ≤ nums[5]=1, yes sorted. Target 0 in [0,1)? yes, search left: right=4. Step 3: left=4, right=4, mid=4, nums[4]=0 == target, return 4.

Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 != 3. Left half [4,5,6,7] sorted (4≤7). Target 3 not in [4,7) so search right: left=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 != 3. Left half [0,1,2] sorted (0≤1). Target 3 not in [0,1) so search right: left=6. Step 3: left=6, right=6, mid=6, nums[6]=2 != 3. Left half [2] sorted (2≤2). Target 3 not in [2,2] so search right: left=7. Step 4: left=7 > right=6, exit loop, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Determine Sorted Half and Search Direction
ladders.desc: Identify which half of current search space is sorted and decide where to search next.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core decision-making step in modified binary search. Given left, right, mid indices and array nums, we determine whether the left half (left to mid) or right half (mid to right) is sorted, and based on that, decide if target is in that sorted half to update search pointers. The intuition is that in a rotated sorted array, at least one half around mid will be sorted (non-decreasing). By checking if nums[left] ≤ nums[mid], we test if left half is sorted (equality handles single element). If true, left half is sorted; else, right half must be sorted. Once we know which half is sorted, we check if target lies within that sorted half's range. For left half sorted: if nums[left] ≤ target < nums[mid], then target is in left half, so set right = mid-1; otherwise, target must be in right half, so set left = mid+1. For right half sorted: if nums[mid] < target ≤ nums[right], then target is in right half, so set left = mid+1; otherwise, target must be in left half, so set right = mid-1. This reduces search space by half each time. Time complexity per call is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the critical logic inside the while loop. Common mistakes: using strict inequalities incorrectly (e.g., target ≤ nums[mid] for left half could include mid, but we already checked mid separately). Real code pattern: if nums[left] <= nums[mid]: if nums[left] <= target < nums[mid]: right = mid-1; else: left = mid+1; else: if nums[mid] < target <= nums[right]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1.

examples.trace: Check left half sorted: nums[0]=4 ≤ nums[3]=7, yes sorted. Check target in left half: nums[0]=4 ≤ target=0? No (0<4), so target not in left half. Thus search right: update left=mid+1=4.

Check left half sorted: nums[4]=0 ≤ nums[5]=1, yes sorted. Check target in left half: nums[4]=0 ≤ target=0? Yes (0=0) and target=0 < nums[5]=1? Yes, so target in left half. Thus search left: update right=mid-1=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Sorted Half in Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Determine which segment (left or right of mid) is sorted in a rotated sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic identifies whether the left half (from left index to mid index) or right half (from mid index to right index) of a rotated sorted array is in sorted order. The intuition relies on the property that in a rotated sorted array with distinct elements, exactly one of these halves will be fully sorted (non-decreasing). To check if the left half is sorted, compare nums[left] and nums[mid]. If nums[left] ≤ nums[mid], then the left half is sorted. The equality case (nums[left] == nums[mid]) occurs when left == mid (single element), which is trivially sorted. If nums[left] > nums[mid], then the left half cannot be sorted because the smallest element of a sorted left half would be at left, but mid is smaller, indicating rotation point lies in left half, so right half must be sorted. This detection is crucial because once we know which half is sorted, we can apply binary search logic on that half. Time complexity is O(1) with simple comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the sorted half information to decide search direction. Common mistakes: assuming both halves might be sorted (possible in non-rotated array, but algorithm still works) or mishandling equal elements (problem assumes distinct). Real code pattern: is_left_sorted = nums[left] <= nums[mid]; if is_left_sorted: # left half sorted; else: # right half sorted.

examples.trace: Compare nums[left]=4 and nums[mid]=7. 4 ≤ 7 is true, so left half [4,5,6,7] is sorted.
Compare nums[left]=0 and nums[mid]=1. 0 ≤ 1 is true, so left half [0,1] is sorted.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search to find target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements classic binary search on a sorted array in ascending order. The intuition is to repeatedly divide the search interval in half by comparing the target with the middle element. If the target equals the middle element, return its index. If target is less than middle, search in the left half; if greater, search in the right half. This works because the array is sorted, ensuring the target, if present, lies entirely within one half. The algorithm uses two pointers, left and right, initially covering the entire array. While left ≤ right, compute mid = (left + right) // 2. If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If target < nums[mid], set right = mid - 1 to search left half. If target > nums[mid], set left = mid + 1 to search right half. If loop ends without finding target, return -1. Time complexity is O(log n) as search space halves each step. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by being the foundational search technique applied once a sorted half is identified. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in pointer updates, integer overflow in mid calculation (use left + (right-left)//2), and incorrect loop condition (use ≤ to include single-element case). Real code pattern: left, right = 0, len(nums)-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] == target: return mid; elif target < nums[mid]: right = mid-1; else: left = mid+1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=3 == target, return 3.

Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=3 < 7, left=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=5 < 7, left=6. Step 3: left=6, right=6, mid=6, nums[6]=6 < 7, left=7. Step 4: left=7 > right=6, exit loop, return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update Pointers in Search
ladders.desc: Compare target with middle element and adjust left/right pointers accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing a target value with the middle element of a current search range and updating the left or right pointer to narrow the search. The intuition is that in a sorted context, this comparison tells us which half of the array to search next. Given left and right pointers defining the current interval [left, right], compute mid index. If target equals nums[mid], we found the element. If target is less than nums[mid], the target must be in the left half (from left to mid-1), so update right = mid - 1. If target is greater than nums[mid], the target must be in the right half (from mid+1 to right), so update left = mid + 1. This operation reduces the search space by approximately half each time. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the fundamental step within binary search loop. Common mistakes: updating pointers incorrectly (e.g., right = mid instead of mid-1, which might cause infinite loops) or not handling equality properly. Real code pattern: mid = (left + right) // 2; if target == nums[mid]: return mid; elif target < nums[mid]: right = mid - 1; else: left = mid + 1.

examples.trace: Compare target=2 with nums[mid]=3. target < nums[mid], so target is in left half. Update right = mid-1 = 2.
Compare target=6 with nums[mid]=5. target > nums[mid], so target is in right half. Update left = mid+1 = 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers how to access elements in an array using indices. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each identifiable by an index. In most programming languages, array indexing is zero-based, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax array[index]. For example, if nums = [10, 20, 30], then nums[0] returns 10, nums[1] returns 20, and nums[2] returns 30. The index must be within the valid range [0, length-1], where length is the number of elements; accessing out of bounds causes errors. This is fundamental because binary search relies heavily on accessing middle elements via indices. Understanding zero-based indexing is crucial for calculating mid indices correctly (e.g., mid = (left + right) // 2). Additionally, arrays support operations like reading elements (O(1) time) and updating elements (O(1) time if index known). This feeds into L4 as the basic mechanism to retrieve nums[mid] for comparison. Common mistakes: using one-based indexing incorrectly, assuming negative indices (which may have special meanings in some languages), or not checking bounds. Real code pattern: element = nums[index]; # where index is an integer between 0 and len(nums)-1.

examples.trace: Array nums has elements: index 0:4, 1:5, 2:6, 3:7, 4:0, 5:1, 6:2. Access nums[3] returns the element at position 3, which is 7.
Access nums[5] returns the element at position 5, which is 1.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Find Pivot Then Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: First, find the pivot index (the smallest element) using binary search, then perform standard binary search in the appropriate sorted half. This separates the rotation handling into two distinct phases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Sorted Rotated Array Using Pivot + Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find pivot index then binary search in appropriate half
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the problem in two distinct phases, each using binary search for O(log n) time. First, we find the pivot index (the smallest element) where the rotation occurs. The pivot divides the array into two sorted halves: left half from pivot to end, and right half from start to pivot-1. Once we have the pivot, we determine which sorted half could contain our target based on comparing target with array boundaries. Then we perform standard binary search in that half. The intuition is that by separating rotation handling from searching, we maintain clean binary search logic in both phases. The pivot finding works by comparing mid element with the rightmost element: if nums[mid] > nums[right], the pivot must be in the right half (since sorted array is broken there); otherwise, it's in the left half including mid. This gives us O(log n) for pivot finding plus O(log n) for searching = O(log n) total. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty/single-element arrays, 2) Off-by-one errors in pivot search termination, 3) Incorrectly determining which half to search after finding pivot. The connection to L1 is that this level combines both helper functions into a complete solution.

Algorithm:
1. Handle edge cases (empty array, single element)
2. Find pivot index using binary search:
   - left = 0, right = n-1
   - while left < right:
     - mid = (left+right)//2
     - if nums[mid] > nums[right]: left = mid+1
     - else: right = mid
   - pivot = left
3. Determine search range:
   - if target >= nums[pivot] and target <= nums[n-1]: search in [pivot, n-1]
   - else: search in [0, pivot-1]
4. Perform standard binary search in chosen range
5. Return index or -1

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace: Step 1: Find pivot. left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 > nums[6]=2 → left=4. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 > nums[6]=2? No (1<2) → right=5. left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[4]=0 > nums[5]=1? No (0<1) → right=4. left=4, right=4 → pivot=4. Step 2: Determine range. target=0, nums[pivot]=0, nums[6]=2 → 0>=0 and 0<=2 → search in [4,6]. Step 3: Binary search in [4,6]. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 > target=0 → right=4. left=4, right=4, nums[4]=0 == target → return 4.

Step 1: Find pivot (same as above) → pivot=4. Step 2: Determine range. target=3, nums[pivot]=0, nums[6]=2 → 3>=0 and 3<=2? No (3>2) → search in [0,3]. Step 3: Binary search in [0,3]. left=0, right=3, mid=1, nums[1]=5 > target=3 → right=0. left=0, right=0, nums[0]=4 > target=3 → right=-1 (loop ends). Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Pivot Index in Sorted Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Binary search to locate the smallest element (rotation point)
ladders.explanation:
This is the critical first phase of the full solution. The pivot is defined as the index of the minimum element in the rotated sorted array, which marks where the original sorted array was rotated. For example, in [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], the pivot is 4 (element 0). Finding this pivot efficiently allows us to then search in a properly sorted subarray. The intuition: in a rotated sorted array, comparing the middle element with the rightmost element tells us which side contains the pivot. If nums[mid] > nums[right], then the pivot must be to the right of mid (because in a sorted array, elements should be ≤ the rightmost). Otherwise, the pivot is at mid or to the left. We use binary search with this comparison to find the pivot in O(log n) time. Common mistakes: 1) Using left <= right instead of left < right causing infinite loops, 2) Comparing with wrong boundary elements, 3) Not handling already-sorted arrays (where pivot is 0). The connection to L0 is that this pivot index is used to determine which sorted half to search.

Algorithm:
1. left = 0, right = n-1
2. while left < right:
   - mid = (left+right)//2
   - if nums[mid] > nums[right]: left = mid+1
   - else: right = mid
3. return left (pivot index)

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 > nums[6]=2 → left=4. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 > nums[6]=2? No (1<2) → right=5. left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[4]=0 > nums[5]=1? No (0<1) → right=4. left=4, right=4 → return 4.

left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[2]=3 > nums[4]=5? No (3<5) → right=2. left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[1]=2 > nums[2]=3? No (2<3) → right=1. left=0, right=1, mid=0, nums[0]=1 > nums[1]=2? No (1<2) → right=0. left=0, right=0 → return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search Comparison Strategy for Rotated Arrays
ladders.desc: Using mid-right comparison to determine pivot location
ladders.explanation:
This is the central insight that makes pivot finding possible with binary search. In a standard sorted array, binary search compares mid with target. Here, we're searching for the pivot (minimum element), so we compare mid with the right boundary to decide which half to recurse into. The intuition: In a rotated sorted array, the rightmost element serves as a reference point. If nums[mid] > nums[right], then the rotation pivot must be to the right of mid. Why? Because in a properly sorted array (without rotation), every element is ≤ the rightmost element. If mid is greater than right, the sorted order is broken between mid and right, so the pivot (where the sorted sequence restarts) lies in that region. Conversely, if nums[mid] ≤ nums[right], then the sequence from mid to right is properly sorted, so the pivot must be at mid or to the left. This single comparison allows us to halve the search space each time. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing with left instead of right (less reliable), 2) Not understanding why this comparison works, 3) Using strict inequality incorrectly. The connection to L1 is that this comparison logic is the heart of the pivot-finding algorithm.

Algorithm concept:
- At each step, compare nums[mid] with nums[right]
- If nums[mid] > nums[right]: search right half (left = mid+1)
- Else: search left half including mid (right = mid)

Time: O(log n) per comparison step, Space: O(1)

examples.trace: mid=3, nums[3]=7, nums[6]=2. Compare: 7 > 2 → true, so pivot is in right half (indices 4-6).
mid=5, nums[5]=1, nums[6]=2. Compare: 1 > 2 → false, so pivot is in left half including mid (indices 4-5).

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search to find target in sorted array
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental searching technique used in the second phase after pivot finding. Binary search works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half on a sorted array. The intuition: because the array is sorted, comparing the middle element with the target tells us which half could contain the target. If the middle element equals the target, we're done. If it's greater, the target must be in the left half (since all elements to the right are even larger). If it's smaller, the target must be in the right half. This reduces the problem size by half each time, giving O(log n) efficiency. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop condition (left <= right vs left < right), 2) Incorrect mid calculation causing overflow, 3) Not updating bounds correctly. The connection to L2 is that this same divide-and-conquer principle is adapted for pivot finding, but with a different comparison strategy.

Algorithm:
1. left = start, right = end
2. while left <= right:
   - mid = (left+right)//2
   - if nums[mid] == target: return mid
   - elif nums[mid] < target: left = mid+1
   - else: right = mid-1
3. return -1

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1)

examples.trace: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=4 == target → return 3.
left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=4 > target=3 → right=2. left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[1]=1 < target=3 → left=2. left=2, right=2, mid=2, nums[2]=2 < target=3 → left=3. left=3 > right=2 → return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Halve Search Range
ladders.desc: Fundamental binary search step: compare element and update bounds
ladders.explanation:
This is the atomic operation at the heart of binary search. Given a current search range [left, right] and a middle index mid, we compare the element at mid with some value (target in standard search, or boundary in pivot finding), then update either left or right to halve the search space. The intuition: each comparison gives us enough information to eliminate half of the remaining elements from consideration. In standard binary search, we compare with target: if nums[mid] < target, we know the target cannot be in indices ≤ mid (since array is sorted ascending), so we set left = mid+1. If nums[mid] > target, we know the target cannot be in indices ≥ mid, so we set right = mid-1. This reduction by half each step is what gives logarithmic time complexity. Common mistakes: 1) Setting bounds incorrectly (e.g., left = mid instead of mid+1), 2) Not handling equality case separately, 3) Using the wrong comparison operator. The connection to L3 is that this operation is repeated in a loop to implement full binary search.

Operation pseudocode:
mid = (left+right)//2
if comparison_condition:
    update left or right appropriately
else:
    update the other bound

Time: O(1) per operation, Space: O(1)

examples.output: left=0, right=4 → mid=2, nums[2]=5 == target → found
examples.trace: Calculate mid = (0+4)//2 = 2. Compare nums[2]=5 with target=5: equal → return found.
mid=2, nums[2]=5 < target=6 → update left = mid+1 = 3. New range [3,4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and comparing values
ladders.explanation:
This is the most basic prerequisite: understanding how to access elements in an array using indices, and how to compare numerical values. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via integer indices starting from 0. For example, in array nums = [4,5,6,7], nums[0] = 4, nums[1] = 5, etc. Comparison operations (<, >, ==, <=, >=) compare the values of two elements. This is fundamental because binary search relies entirely on comparing the middle element's value with other values to decide the next step. The intuition: without the ability to access any element by index in constant time O(1), binary search wouldn't be possible. Similarly, without comparisons, we couldn't determine which half of the array to search next. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Confusing indices with values, 2) Using wrong comparison operators, 3) Not understanding 0-based indexing. The connection to L4 is that these operations are used in every step of the compare-and-halve operation.

Basic operations:
- Access: element = nums[index]
- Compare: if element1 < element2:
- Update index: index = new_value

Time: O(1) for access/comparison, Space: O(1)

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30, 3→40. Accessing index 2 gives value 30.
Get nums[1]=20, nums[3]=40. Compare: 20 < 40 evaluates to true.
